cards.title: Your AI Has Amnesia: The Untapped Goldmine of Personal AI Memory
cards.thesis: People are fed up with AI tools forgetting their context and conversations the moment they switch apps, forcing them to constantly re-explain themselves. All the prompts and chats that reveal how you think and work are currently fragmented across different services, creating a huge missed opportunity for deeply personalized AI experiences.

evidence.text: What annoys people most with AI today is 'having to explain myself every time I swap providers. Claude has no clue to what happened on ChatGPT'.

Your 'AI exhaust' – every prompt, chat, and newsletter you go through – says a lot about how you think and work, but right now it's just sitting in 10 different tools doing nothing. People wonder if they could pool this data to get something useful back, like a model that actually knows them.

There's a problem with 'naive RAG' (Retrieval Augmented Generation, an AI technique for finding relevant information) systems: they grab relevant text chunks but lose the connections between them. When dealing with real-world systems like documentation or APIs (Application Programming Interfaces, which let different software talk to each other), understanding how things link up is more important than just finding similar words.
A user's custom email triage system through Claude broke after a connector update, highlighting how fragile current AI integrations for personal workflows can be.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's complaining that AI tools like Claude forget your whole conversation history the second you jump to ChatGPT, making you re-explain everything. Your personal 'AI exhaust' – all those prompts and chats that show how *you* think and work – is just sitting fragmented across a dozen apps. The move is to build a personal AI memory layer, maybe a browser extension or a desktop app, that quietly observes your interactions across *all* your AI tools. It doesn't just copy text, but learns your context and intent, then automatically feeds that personalized understanding to whatever AI you're currently using. Ship a basic 'context bridge' that just watches your active tab and offers to summarize it to your current AI chat, and you'll own the frustrated-AI-user market.

cards.title: Claude Design is Hot: The Race to Turn AI Prototypes into Production-Ready UI Kits
cards.thesis: Claude Design is blowing up, with builders using it to quickly generate user interface (UI) prototypes. While people are sharing design inspirations and basic converters, there's a clear scramble to make these AI-generated designs immediately usable in specific coding frameworks, moving beyond just raw HTML output.

evidence.text: There's a lot of discussion and interest around Claude Design.
People are sharing collections of 'ready-to-use design system inspirations in DESIGN.md format' that you can 'Drop one in, scaffold a full UI in one shot' using Claude.
A plugin exists to 'convert Claude-generated HTML design prototypes into SwiftUI Views' for Xcode.

Builders are creating 'drop-in AGENTS.md' files to make coding agents (like Claude Code, Cursor, Gemini CLI) 'behave like a senior engineer instead of an eager intern,' forcing better output and preventing unwanted changes.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's dropping `DESIGN.md` files into Claude to get a UI, but the output still needs a ton of manual cleanup to fit a specific framework or design system beyond basic HTML. The chance here is to create a component library (think `shadcn` for AI) where each component is meticulously documented and structured specifically for an agent like Claude to understand and generate *perfectly* into a specific framework like React or SwiftUI. The first person to ship a high-quality, AI-optimized component set for a popular framework (like a 'Claude-Native React UI Kit') will own the market for AI-generated, production-ready UIs.

cards.title: AI's Overprotective Mom Problem: Build a 'Digital Dirty Work' Proxy
cards.thesis: AI models are becoming so cautious they're frustrating developers, refusing legitimate tasks like parsing HTML or basic code generation, often citing 'security' or 'usage policy' violations. This over-censoriousness, combined with increasing difficulty in programmatic web access (like using automated scripts to get data from websites), creates a huge bottleneck for anyone trying to build with AI agents.

evidence.text: Claude Code Opus 4.7 keeps checking for malware during development, and refused a task to parse HTML because it believed I was bypassing security measures. I believe AI needs to be supportive, not obsessive.
Claude Code is unable to respond to this request, which appears to violate our Usage Policy. Users are hitting restrictions.
I'm getting depressed day by day about what's coming with AI constantly improving. Companies will prioritize speed over careful work, and my efforts might not matter.

For the first time in a long time, I need to query a search engine programmatically, and found that most of them block the use of curl (a common tool for automated web requests). How do people solve this problem?

cards.opportunity: AI models are getting so cautious they're blocking legitimate dev work, like parsing HTML or even simple code generation, and programmatic web access is a nightmare. There's a massive opening for a 'digital dirty work' proxy — a service where you send your AI's blocked requests (like 'scrape X' or 'parse Y'), it executes them in a sandboxed environment (a controlled, isolated space), sanitizes the output, and feeds the clean data back to your AI. This lets builders get around AI's over-censoriousness and web blocking, fast-tracking agent development without actually running risky code directly.

cards.title: Stop Explaining Your AI Projects. Start SHOWING Them.
cards.thesis: Builders are going all-in on AI, even considering career breaks to experiment with advanced multi-agent systems where AIs talk to other AIs. But there's a massive gap: no easy way for them to visually explain or document these complex AI workflows to others, which means their cutting-edge work often goes unnoticed or misunderstood.

evidence.text: Show HN: MDV – a Markdown superset for docs, dashboards, and slides with data. (156 engagement)
Ask HN: Anyone know of that 'levels of AI programming' blog post? It started at 0... and went to around 7 where LLMs direct other LLMs and no human is involved.
Ask HN: Is it worth taking a year off? I'd love to spend a year just experimenting with AI, building stuff, and documenting the whole thing.
TIL: You can read Claude Code quota locally (no scraping, no API) by checking a local settings file to see your usage limits.

cards.opportunity: With everyone diving deep into multi-agent AI systems, trying to figure out 'levels of AI programming' and 'documenting the whole thing,' a huge gap exists: how do you actually *show* what these complex AI workflows are doing? Nobody's built a simple, visual documentation tool specifically for AI agent architectures – think a living diagram or interactive Markdown (a simple text formatting language) that maps out how LLMs (Large Language Models, the 'brains' of many AIs) direct other LLMs. If you ship a simple drag-and-drop or Markdown-based tool that lets builders visualize and explain their AI project's internal 'AI levels' and agent interactions, you'll immediately capture the market of builders desperate to showcase their advanced AI experiments and ease career anxieties.
